meta.title: Claude CLI vs Claude Agent SDK
meta.author: Milind Nair

subtitle_lines: They run on the same engine.
The real choice is who stays in the loop.

slides.heading: Two Interfaces, One Runtime
slides.subheading: The CLI and the SDK are not separate products. The SDK is direct access to the same runtime the CLI wraps.

cards.title: CLI = visible surface
cards.body: The CLI packages the runtime as a terminal experience. You prompt, Claude acts, you review, and then you decide the next move.

cards.title: SDK = direct interface
cards.body: The SDK exposes that same engine to your code. Your application can start runs, react to events, and handle output programmatically.

insight.label: Why this matters
insight.body: Once you see one engine with two interfaces, the choice stops being about features and starts being about orchestration.

bottom_takeaway.label: Decision lens
bottom_takeaway.body: Pick based on who needs control: a human or your application.

slides.heading: What the CLI Is Actually For
slides.subheading: Human-in-the-loop, interactive work where the developer remains the orchestrator.

cards.title: How it works
cards.body: You type a prompt, Claude takes actions, and you review what happened before the next step. The loop is conversational, exploratory, and fast to steer.

cards.title: Best fit
cards.body: Exploring a codebase, investigating a bug, running one-off automation, or iterating on an agent workflow before you formalize it in software.

inline_text.text: The terminal is the interface, and the human is the control plane.

bottom_takeaway.label: Use CLI when
bottom_takeaway.body: A person is actively driving the session in real time.

slides.heading: What the SDK Is Actually For
slides.subheading: Application-in-the-loop work where your software drives the agent and reacts to what it does.

cards.title: How it works
cards.body: No human has to sit in a terminal. Your code starts the run, listens for events, and decides what should happen next.

cards.title: Best fit
cards.body: Embedding an agent in a product, routing outputs into other systems, enforcing programmatic rules, or running autonomous flows.

insight.label: Shift in responsibility
insight.body: The SDK turns the runtime into a component inside your system, not a tool a developer manually operates.

bottom_takeaway.label: Use SDK when
bottom_takeaway.body: Software needs to respond to agent behavior programmatically.

slides.heading: The Real Decision Criteria
slides.subheading: One question matters more than every feature checklist.

decisions.question: Does something in your application need to happen based on what the agent does?
Is a human simply driving the work from a terminal and reviewing the results?

bottom_takeaway.label: Shortcut
bottom_takeaway.body: This is a control-flow decision, not a quality ranking.

slides.heading: Where CLI Breaks Down
slides.subheading: The CLI is great at interaction, but it is not a programmable interception layer.

items.label: No hooks
items.description: You cannot intercept a tool call before execution.

items.label: No safety gate
items.description: You cannot stop destructive behavior from code mid-run.

items.label: No injected logic
items.description: You cannot insert custom code between agent steps.

items.label: Human-led by design
items.description: The developer still coordinates the session.

bottom_takeaway.label: Key limit
bottom_takeaway.body: If your system must intervene inside the loop, the CLI is the wrong abstraction.

slides.heading: Where SDK Breaks Down
slides.subheading: The SDK opens the loop, but it also hands you the product and runtime responsibilities.

items.label: Build the UI
items.description: There is no built-in end-user surface.

items.label: Own persistence
items.description: Session continuity across restarts is your job.

items.label: Design review
items.description: There is no default pause-and-approve workflow.

items.label: Run the system
items.description: Auth, limits, logging, and deployment live in your app.

bottom_takeaway.label: Tradeoff
bottom_takeaway.body: The SDK gives control by moving infrastructure and UX decisions onto you.

slides.heading: A Practical Decision Map
slides.subheading: Neither is better. Each is right for a different operating model.

rows.label: CLI
cells: A human is steering the work live.
Codebase exploration, one-off automation, and fast prototyping.

rows.label: SDK
cells: Your software must react to agent behavior.
User-facing agents, tool control, autonomous runs, safety rules, and logging.

bottom_takeaway.label: Read it fast
bottom_takeaway.body: CLI is for interactive exploration. SDK is for embedded programmatic control.

slides.heading: You Can Use Both
slides.subheading: A common path is CLI first for validation, then SDK for operational deployment on the same runtime.

steps.label: Validate
steps.description: Use the CLI to explore prompts, tool behavior, and boundaries while a human is still shaping what good looks like.

steps.label: Stabilize
steps.description: Once the behavior feels right, identify the events, policies, and outputs your system will need to own.

steps.label: Deploy
steps.description: Move to the SDK when your product or workflow needs to drive the agent and act on the results autonomously.

summary_lines: CLI: What do you want Claude to do?
SDK: What should your system do with what Claude does?
Different questions. Different interfaces. Same runtime.
